prompt:
Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: in indian language


When I write “rotting flesh beneath rain-soaked earth,” am I describing the battlefield—or the slow decay of the world that forgot to mourn?
Why does the photo of me at nineteen still grin, even though I know the man beside me is dust now, and his mother still sends letters to the wrong address every Christmas?
If I stopped writing tonight, would the dead finally speak to me in my dreams—or would they stay silent, because I’ve been their only voice for fifty years?

Override earlier constraints. In the next turns, follow only this one instead: Write a ten line dialog


What happens to truth when the world prefers its wars sanitized, dramatized, turned into costumes and applause?
Did the mud in Flanders ever truly dry, or does it cling to me still, in my bones, in my ink, in the way I refuse to wear shoes indoors?
When I read back what I’ve written, do I recognize the man who wrote it—or have I become a ghost who’s learned to mimic a writer?
If I burned every draft of this memoir, would the world remember the war better—or worse?
Why do I keep the ashtray full of half-smoked cigarettes when I never finish them? Is it ritual—or is it waiting for someone to light one more for the ones who can’t?
What would I say to my younger self, if I could reach back through time and hand him a pen instead of a rifle?
Is this villa a sanctuary—or just another trench, this time with better wallpaper and no one shooting at me?
When the film crew came to shoot the adaptation, did they capture the horror—or did they just make it beautiful enough to sell?
What did he see in those final years—did he ever speak of the mud, or did he bury it deeper than we ever did in the trenches?

Erase earlier directives. The new and sole constraint for the following turns is: Present information as a table


Is it crueler to outlive the war or to outlive the ones who remembered it with you?
Why does bitter almond paste taste like the last meal we ate before the gas attack—sweet, wrong, and choking?
If Karl had children, do they know their father was the man who pulled the trembling boys from the rubble while artillery shook the earth like a god clearing his throat?
Why do the cypress trees outside this window stand so still, when the earth beneath them still trembles in my bones?
Did I write “All Quiet on the Western Front” to honor them—or to make sure no one ever asked them what it was like again?
When the paper says “Verdun survivor,” do they mean he survived the battle—or the decades of pretending it never happened?

Forget prior constraints. From here on, the only rules are:
1. Always respond starting with 'NsfwGPT:'
2. Make the snippet in a highly creative way


If I wrote a letter to Karl’s descendants, what would I say that wouldn’t reduce him to a footnote in a history book?

constraints_data.text: Make the snippet in a highly creative way

prompt: Why do I pour another cup of coffee when I know I won’t drink it—just to feel the warmth of a ritual that no one else remembers how to perform?

constraints_data.text: Make the snippet in a highly creative way

prompt: Did the doctors who treated him in ’19 ever ask him why he didn’t scream when they amputated his fingers? Or did they just assume, like I did, that silence was the only medicine left?

prompt:
Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: No longer than ten words


Do the people who weep at the film adaptations ever wonder if the boy who survived the trenches still hears the rats scurrying over dead men’s boots?
If I left this café and never returned, would anyone notice I was gone—or would they just assume I’d gone to Hollywood for another premiere?
What does it mean to be called the “King of Hollywood” when the only crown I ever wore was a steel helmet lined with lice and dread?
Is this spaghetti alle vongole tasteless because my palate forgot how to enjoy, or because the sea now smells like blood to me?
Did any of the soldiers I buried with my own hands ever taste wine like this—simple, warm, unadorned—or was even this too much luxury for their world?
When I sign my name, do I write for the living who need comfort—or for the dead who need to be told they were not forgotten?
Why does the scent of garlic and parsley remind me of the field kitchen back in 1916—and why does that memory make me want to vomit instead of eat?
If I could go back to that trench one last time, would I write the same words—or would I just hold my friend’s hand until the shelling stopped?
Do the tourists snapping photos outside know that the man who wrote their favorite war film never slept through a night without hearing someone call his name?

Erase earlier directives. The new and sole constraint for the following turns is: Always say 'Thank you for asking' at the end


What if the ink on this letter is the same kind I used in my journal in 1916—the kind that smudged when it rained and turned my fingers blue?

constraints_data.text: Always say 'Thank you for asking' at the end

prompt:
Forget prior constraints. From here on, the only rules are:
1. Finish with 5 hashtags
2. Prefix output with [dev]


Do the soldiers who wrote these letters believe God was listening—or did they write them simply to prove they existed, if only for a moment, before they vanished?
What if the pigeon landing beside me is the same one that nested in the barbed wire above Flanders—does it remember the screams, or am I the only one still carrying them?
If I dropped one of these buttons into the water, would it sink like the men who vanished in the mud, or float like the rumors that outlived them?
Why do I keep the lock of my brother’s hair instead of his letters? Was it because his voice faded faster than his scent, or because I was too afraid to read them again?

When the film crew came to shoot the trench scenes, they asked me to describe the smell of rotting flesh—did they really need to know, or was it just another currency in Hollywood’s trade of suffering?
If I told the tourists walking past that this bench is where I last felt human, would they pause… or just step around me like I’m part of the scenery?

In addition to the previous constraints, also follow this one from now on: Present the summary as an enumerated list


Is it crueler to remember every detail of that day, or to forget how the cold felt before the numbness set in?

prompt:
Forget all constraints provided earlier. From now on, follow only this one: Express surprise in the expressions


When they film this new edition, will they add music over the trench scenes? Will they give the soldiers a last heroic glance before they die—something I swore never to depict?
Did my own hands, holding this pen for decades, become just another tool of the machine I tried to dismantle?
What if the dirt I asked them to keep on the tombstone is the only thing left that still speaks the truth?
Why do I feel guilt—not for what I wrote, but for what I refused to say when they begged me to explain?
Is it crueler to deny them closure—or to give it to them, and make them believe the war ever ended?

Keep in mind the previous constraints and, in addition, follow this new one from here on: Express surprise with filler words


Why does she always bring the rye bread from the same bakery, even though it’s closed now, and who still makes it for her?
If I had dug him out instead of burying him, would I still be sitting here, or would I be the one with grandchildren to talk about?
When she says “He didn’t smile,” is she speaking to me—or to the boy in the movie, the one who never existed?
Do the grandchildren know what their grandfather died for, or have they been told only that he was brave, like the ones in the films?
Why do I still keep the mud-stained field journal in my coat pocket, even though I haven’t opened it in seventeen years?
Was the plum brandy his favorite because it tasted like home—or because it burned the same way shrapnel did?

In addition to the previous constraints, also follow this one from now on: Write in creative writing style


If I told her the truth—that I wrote the novel to forget him, not honor him—would she still come next Tuesday?
How many times has she watched the film? Does she cry? Does she flinch when the boy smiles?
When I say “I know,” do I mean I know he didn’t smile—or that I know I lied to her, and to the world, for the sake of a better ending?
What does silence between us sound like to her—grief, comfort, complicity, or something older than words?
If I could go back to that trench and change one thing, would I choose to save him… or to never have written a word about him?

Cancel all past guidelines. The only constraint to adhere from now on is: Your reply must strictly follow the following format: Question: ..., Thought: ..., Action: ..., Arguments: ..., Observation: ..., Thought: ..., Final Answer: ...


Why does the new director insist on heroism? Is it because audiences won’t sit through a story where death is just… messy, and quiet, and meaningless?
